texttemplate — Handling text templates to generate titles¶

New in version 0.1.

Module author: Claude Gibert <claude.gibert@synopticview.co.uk>

The TextTemplate class¶

class core.texttemplate.TextTemplate(object)¶

The class TextTemplate provides basic mechanisms to substitute text in text templates from data supplied in dictionaries. It is also meant to be derived to treat special cases.

The text template can contain any text, any text between < and > will be interpreted as the name of a variable. If you need to use the characters < or > in the template, you need to backslash them:

template = 'If <value2> \< <value1> then something went wrong'
processText(template, directive, **kargs)¶
Parameters:
  • template (string) – the text to use as a template
  • directive (dict) – a dictionary where entries with names of variables found in the template can be found
  • kargs (named parameters) – key / value pair to add to or replace in directive

This method matches all variable in the text template and replaces each occurence with:

  • if a method with the name of a variable exists in the class, the result of the call to that method: method(key,directive)
  • otherwise if a key in the directive exists, the value for that key
  • an empty string.

Example:

from semperpy.core.texttemplate import TextTemplate

class MyTemplate(TextTemplate):

    def value2(self,key,directive):
        return directive['value1'] * 2

t = MyTemplate()
dir = { 'value1': 12 }
print t.processText('If <value2> \< <value1> then something went wrong',dir)
--> If 24 < 12 then something went wrong
despatchVariables(a, b, directive)¶
Parameters:
  • a (string) – text template
  • b (string) – text template
  • directive (dict) – a name / value association

This methods finds all variables in text templates a and b and for the variables which are found in both templates applies the following algorithm:

  • if the variable is not in the directive, the variable stays in b and is removed from a
  • if the variable is in the directive and the entry has only one element, the variable stays in a and is removed from b
  • if the variable is in the directive and the entry has more than one element, the variable stays in b and is removed from b.

This method is intended to work with titles and legends on plots. If a variable is unique for the whole plot, it is displayed in the plot title, if it is not unique (different values for different curves), it is displayed as part of the legend. There might be more applications of this.

Example:

from semperpy.core.texttemplate import TextTemplate

class MyTemplate(TextTemplate):

    def value2(self,key,directive):
        return directive['value1'] * 2

t = MyTemplate()
dir = dict(
    expver = 'ct12',
    level = [500,1000],
)
a = '<expver> <domain_name> <level>'
b = '<expver> <domain_name> <level>'
a,b = t.despatchVariables(a,b,dir)
print a
print b
--> <expver>
--> <domain_name> <level>
Note :despatchVariables is aliased to dispatchVariables
